coredata.dc:title: Single-shot ‘turn-off’ optical probe for rapid detection of paraoxon-ethyl pesticide on vegetable utilising fluorescence carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Melissa May Fung
Ginjom, Irine Runnie
Ng, Sing Muk
coredata.dc:description: The utilisation of pesticides has become more intensified as to fulfil the demand of food for the ever-growing world population. It leads to the risk of pesticides residues leaching to the environment, which subsequently reflects to the needs of having effective and economical detection and monitoring approach. In view of this, a work has been performed as reported in this paper on the development of a simple and economical single-shot optical probe for the detection of paraoxon-ethyl pesticide on real vegetable samples. The probe adopted carbon dots as sensing receptor that has been synthesised in-house via simple acid carbonisation of sucrose. The carbon dots isolated showed strong yellow fluorescence with the maximum emission at 524nm when excited at 423nm. Upon edition with paraoxon-ethyl, the emission was quenched accordingly with concentration dependent manner. The sensing protocol was optimised and validated, while the analytical characteristic was evaluated as an effective single-shot probe that can detect paraoxon-ethyl extracted from spiked vegetable leaves. The probe has achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.22±0.02μM and a dynamic linear range up to 5.80mM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Sensing
Quenching
Fluorescence
Pesticide
Food safety

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance, (b) excitation and (c) emission spectrum of CDs derived from carbonisation of sucrose by concentrated H3PO4.
SEM of the CDs isolated from this study.
XPS spectra for CDs and the insets are the enlarged regions of the carbon and oxygen peaks.
The separation of CDs into acetone from the initial mother-liquor solution.

The bar chart shows the enhancement of fluorescence intensity upon addition of pure acetone at a 1:1 vol ratio of CDs stock and acetone. Inset is the effect of acetone up to 400μL towards the fluorescence signal.
The quenching in fluorescence intensity in the presence of different concentrations of paraoxon-ethyl ranging from 0 to 5.8 Mm.

The most effective quenching range of the fluorescence intensity in the present of paraoxon-ethyl. Inset shows the linear range on the relationship between intensity quenching and paraoxon-ethyl concentration.
Comparison of analytical performance of several sensors reported to detect paraoxon pesticides.
Relative errors recorded for the assay in the presence of interference species at the concentration ratio (paraoxon-ethyl: foreign species) of 1:1 and 1:2.

head.ce:title: Single-shot ‘turn-off’ optical probe for rapid detection of paraoxon-ethyl pesticide on vegetable utilising fluorescence carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilisation of pesticides has become more intensified as to fulfil the demand of food for the ever-growing world population. It leads to the risk of pesticides residues leaching to the environment, which subsequently reflects to the needs of having effective and economical detection and monitoring approach. In view of this, a work has been performed as reported in this paper on the development of a simple and economical single-shot optical probe for the detection of paraoxon-ethyl pesticide on real vegetable samples. The probe adopted carbon dots as sensing receptor that has been synthesised in-house via simple acid carbonisation of sucrose. The carbon dots isolated showed strong yellow fluorescence with the maximum emission at 524nm when excited at 423nm. Upon edition with paraoxon-ethyl, the emission was quenched accordingly with concentration dependent manner. The sensing protocol was optimised and validated, while the analytical characteristic was evaluated as an effective single-shot probe that can detect paraoxon-ethyl extracted from spiked vegetable leaves. The probe has achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.22±0.02μM and a dynamic linear range up to 5.80mM.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticide is generally defined by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of the United Nations as any substance that is intentionally applied to prevent, destroy or control pests in the form of animal or plant from interfering with the crops production chain [1]. These include insecticides, herbicides, fungicides and various pest control substances. Due to the ever growing world population that has subsequently caused the increase on demand for foods and shelters, the usage of pesticides has intensified with the intention to boost up the production yield of crops. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has estimated that the world has consumed approximately 5.2 billion pounds in total of pesticides in both in the year 2006 and 2007, with around 40% of the amount were contributed from herbicides [2]. With such as large amount used, this reflects directly some concerns over the amount of residues leaching into the environment and food chains in the ecosystem. To worsen the scenario, the application methods of pesticides to the crops are often ineffective. For instance, the use of knapsack sprayer can only apply approximately 20% of the pesticides to the plants with only less than 1% of the active compounds contribute to pest control and the rest will contaminate the surrounding [3]. In addition, higher dosage of pesticide is often needed after several rounds of application due to the emergence of resistant pest strains. Accidental or intentional discharge of pesticide rinses can cause direct exposure to the non-targeted organisms in the surrounding.

Pesticides residues even at low amount can cause serious effect on the nervous systems of living organisms such as domestic livestock, fish, birds, wildlife and humans once they entered the food chain [4]. This is due to the irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) that leads to critical neurotoxic effects [5,6]. Other symptoms are such as causing seizures, paralysis, respiratory failure, and chronic health effects due to central nervous system damage, DNA damage, immune system deficits, deformities and cancer development [7–10]. This reflects a real need to monitor closely on the level of pesticides in the environment and crop products, where fast mitigation can be activated to reduce the risk of exposure for non-targeted organisms including humans.

Currently, there are several established laboratory-based methods used for the detection of pesticide residues. These are mostly using conventional instrumentation for detection such as chromatography [11,12], electrochemical [13,14], surface acoustic wave [15] and ELISA [16,17] based analyses. The methods have good reliability, sensitivity and selectivity, but nonetheless the accessibility to the analysis and practicality of the methods for on-site monitoring are in fact limited. One of the major reasons is due to the high cost involved in the instrumentation, operation, and consumables, which make it unaffordable for general public. Only certain laboratories will have these facilities and any sample that requires analysis will need to be transported in and later queueing up for analysis. This often prolongs the duration from sampling to obtaining the analysis result. Real time monitoring is also not possible since the instruments are not designed for robust on-site operation. Thus, many efforts have been engaged to develop simple, cost effective and reliable analytical probes as alternative for the detection of pesticides. For instance, Fan et al. has reported the used of sensor arrays for the detection of multicomponent pesticides [18], while others have developed nanoparticles based biosensors for the detection of various pesticides [19–21]. There are also reports on detection probes for pesticides based on immune-chip technology [22,23] and electrochemical sensing system [13,24].

Optical based analytical probe offers a good option of analysis since it involves inexpensive reagents, simple instrumentation, rapid response, ease to perform, and portable. The option becomes more attractive when combined with nanomaterials science, especially nanoparticles that show several unique properties as compared to its bulk format despite similarity in chemical composition. This includes the shift of the surface-area-to-volume ratio to an extremely large value that leads to a drastic change in the nature of the interface chemistry [25,26]. Often, target molecules are reported to interact and bind more easily on the surface active sites when in nanoparticles format [27]. In addition, the radii that is smaller than the exciton Bohr radius has led to the rise of the luminescence property [28]. Of the different types, carbon based nanoparticles or more commonly known as carbon dots (CDs) is one of the latest class that have attracted much attention among researchers. CDs have less toxic nature as compared to conventional quantum dots, simple to prepare, more economical in terms of starting materials and synthesis facilities, and the starting materials can be derived even from greener options. For instance, CDs can be isolated economically from sago barks, an agricultural by-product via simple thermal carbonisation approach [29]. Due to strong luminescence property, CDs are widely reported to be adopted in bio-imaging and optical sensing applications [30]. Thus, the hybrid of optical detection method with CDs as detection receptor has good potential to be developed into analytical probe for the detection of pesticides with features such a readily available, easy to perform, affordable, ease of disposal after use and environmental friendly [31].

This study reports the development of a single-shot optical probe using CDs as detection reagent for quantitative determination of paraoxon-ethyl pesticide. The motivations were to develop an probe that is robust, sensitive, cost-effective, target for real samples, and easy to perform. The CDs were prepared from simple acid carbonisation of sucrose using phosphoric acid under optimised condition. The luminescence response of the CDs in the presence of paraoxon-ethyl was characterised. The system was used to detect paraoxon-ethyl that was extracted from spiked leaves. In this case, the method was validated using conventional high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).

All chemicals purchased were of analytical grade and used without further purification unless otherwise stated. Sucrose (C12H22O11) and phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85%) were purchased from Bendosen (Malaysia). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Unichem (Malaysia). Acetone was purchased from EMSURE, Merck (Germany). Paraoxon-ethyl was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Malaysia). All aqueous solution was prepared using ultrapure water (18.2M Ω, 25°C) of Millipore Milli-Q system. HPLC study was conducted using HPLC grade reagents and solvents.

Fluorescence measurements were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (CARY Eclipse Varian). Sample was placed in a quartz cuvette of four clear windows and a path length of 1.0cm for measurements. Both emission and excitation slits were set at 10nm. UV–vis absorbance measurements were recorded using a UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 50 Conc). Mettler Toledo SevenEasy pH metre was used for accurate determination of the pH value of the solution. Forced air drying oven (TUFF/Malaysia/TFAC-136) was used to control the temperature of carbonisation process during the synthesis of CDs. Validation analysis was performed using HPLC with PDA detector (Waters/USA Quaternary 600E) via reversed phase, 150mm, LC-18, 5μm column. Isocratic elution was used for the HPLC system and measured at temperature of 30°C. The mobile phase was acetonitrile: water (50:50) for paraoxon-ethyl analysis and the wavelength of UV–vis detector was set at 270nm. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurement and SEM imaging of the CDs sample were outsourced to SIRIM, a premier industrial research and technology organisation in Malaysia that is wholly-owned by the Malaysian Ministry of Finance Incorporated.

The synthesis was performed using the previous method with slight modification as reported by this group [32]. Stock solution of sucrose (10%) was first prepared in ultrapure water and mixed with concentrated H3PO4 (85%) with a volume-to-volume ratio of 3:7 (sucrose: H3PO4) in a Pyrex beaker. After the mixture was homogenised by gentle stirring, the beaker was incubated in an oven (preheated to around 80°C) for 30min. The mixture had turned into light brownish colour after the incubation and was cooled to room temperature. In order to deactivate the carbonisation process, NaOH (1.0M) was added to neutralise the mixture (around pH 7). The neutralised sample was then transferred into a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 4400rpm (3000×g) for 30min. The CDs were collected together in the aliquot because small colloidal particles in the nanometre range can remain in the aliquot while larger particles will sink to the bottom of the tube. In order to purify the CDs from the salts produced during neutralisation, solvent extraction method was performed using acetone with 1:1 vol-to-volume ratio. The mixture was shaken thoroughly on a vortex for 30min and was left to settle for another 30min to equilibrate. Top layer supernatant was collected and placed in a separate centrifuge tube. The solution was then concentrated via vacuum concentrator (LABCONCO) for at least 6h followed by overnight lyophilisation. The solid product was collected and stored in tightly sealed vial. A CDs stock of 50mgmL−1 was prepared with ultrapure water for further analyses.

The probe is based on the degree of fluorescence quenching of the CDs in the presence of known amount of paraoxon-ethyl, corrected against the fluorescence intensity of the blank CDs solution. The blank consisted of 100μL of CDs from stock solution and 100μL of acetone that was later added with ultrapure water to make the final volume of 2.0mL. Stock solution of paraoxon-ethyl was made in acetone with the final concentration of 0.12M. In constructing the calibration curve, known volume of paraoxon-ethyl ranging from 10 to 110μL (interval of 10μL) was added respectively in blank solution. The solution was mixed and left for 1min before the fluorescence intensity was recorded from 450 to 700nm. Calibration curve was constructed on the peak wavelength of band, which was at 524nm.

The feasibility of the single-shot optical probe using CDs was tested using real sample, Bok Choy a type of vegetables commonly consumed by the local public at Malaysia. In this study, Bok Choy was obtained from the local fresh market and the leaves were first cleaned with ultrapure water and left dry at ambient condition. Paraoxon-ethyl working solution (3.63mM) was used to spike the leaves by soaking at a ratio of 1.0mL per g of leaves. Control was taken for leave that was not spiked with the working solution. The spiked leaves were left 24h before 1g of the leave sample was minced and soaked in 2.0mL of acetone for 10min under gentle stirring. The extract was collected and the concentration of paraoxon-ethyl was determined using the standard protocol of the CDs probe developed in this study. The same batch of aliquot was tested with HPLC for validation purpose. All analyses were performed in triplicates and average value was used for reporting.

The strong absorbance band ranging from 260 to 310nm record from the CDs was ascribed to the π–π∗ transition of C=C, where such similar peak was also observed by other reported CDs [34]. This confirmed that the sucrose has undergone chemical decomposition and the formation of new products in the presence of acid even at a mild temperature of 80°C. The change in colour of the solution to light brownish was further evidence that the sucrose has been converted into carbon rich residues. Both results match the commonly reported observation during the carbonisation of organic precursors such as biomass, where it involved hydrolysis, polymerisation and aromatisation processes that ended up in the formation of carbon-rich residues [35]. The CDs have homogenous morphology as recorded by the SEM imaging (Fig. 2 ), with estimated average size of 10nm for the CDs.

The CDs isolated were observed to be readily water soluble due to the rich hydrophilic terminals on the surface of the CDs after treatment with H3PO4. Acids are known to oxidise the surface of CDS and subsequently introduce chemical functionalities such as the carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on it [36]. This was further supported by the XPS result (Fig. 3 ) that shows high density of oxygen content on the CDs. Besides, carbon nanoparticles are mostly reported to be water soluble due to their small size that can form colloidal solution. Majority of the CDs was composed of carbon (66%), while the remaining were oxygen (32%) and each 1% for nitrogen and phosphorus. The satellite peak for carbon suggested the formation of extended delocalised electrons (π-π*)

In this study, the salt was formed due to the addition of base to neutralise the sample. The presence of high salt content will not be favourable to many further studies and applications. Thus has been removed in this study via salt-induced phase separation using acetone. CDs isolated in this study were readily soluble in both water and acetone. However, due to the high concentration of salt, it was possible to form 2 phases between acetone and water (Fig. 4 ). The highly ionic salt species will remain in the water mother-liquor, while the CDs that were less ionic have partitioned into the acetone phase. Although no qualitative analysis is performed to identify the extraction efficiency, it is of high confident that most of the CDs have been transferred to the acetone as the mother-liquor was observed to be white in colour, while the acetone layer turned from clear to be very yellowish as shown in Fig. 4. Acetone is quite volatile and the removal from the CDs has been easily achieved via evaporation.

The detection signal of the probe was taken based on the changes in the fluorescence profile in the presence of paraoxon-ethyl. In this study, the addition of paraoxon-ethyl into the CDs detection system has caused quenching in the fluorescence intensity with a concentration dependent manner (Fig. 6 ). The sensing mechanism is suggested to be the energy transfer of the fluorescence due to the enhancement of dynamic quenching between CDs and paraoxon-ethyl that are attracted on the surface of the CDs. The probability of the fluorescence quenching due to the formation of new origin is low since there was no shifting in the emission signal observed. This indicates that no change in the initial band gap of the fluorescence origin in the presence of paraoxon-ethyl. The XPS result (Fig. 3) shows high density of oxygen content on the surface of the CDs. This is expected as the surface can undergo oxidation by the acid used during the synthesis stage. This oxidised surface can form significant interaction with the phosphate group of the paraoxon-ethyl, aligning molecules on the surface and promote better non-radiant energy transfer processes. The fluorescence can be quenched effectively by the paraoxon-ethyl as evidenced from Fig. 6.

There are several types of organophosphate pesticides that have high probability to co-exist together with paraoxon-ethyl. In the study, two other same category pesticides that are commonly used in agriculture activities, the methyl parathion and malathion respectively were selected for a brief interference study on the proposed probe. The intention was to investigate the degree of interference caused by other pesticides with similar functionality, which eventually can lead to false positive result. Table 2 shows that both the foreign species did not interfere seriously with the probe with an evaluated error below 5% when the concentrations of interfering species were increased even up to a fold. One of the possible suggestions is due to the different in the electronegativity on the phosphate terminal for paraoxon-ethyl that consisting an oxygen moiety with higher electronegativity compared to the sulphur moieties on both methyl parathion and malathion. CDs treated with acid often has highly oxidised surface that is rich in functional groups such as hydroxide and carboxylic group. These groups are high in electron density and expected to promote stronger and more effective with terminals that of high electronegativity as compared to those with lower electronegativity.

Single-shot ‘turn-off’ optical probe has been tested to detect paraoxon-ethyl residue from real vegetable samples (Bok Choy) that were spiked with standard paraoxon-ethyl. Non-spiked Bok Choy was used as reference. The result of the probe was validated using conventional HPLC, with detection at 270nm and monitored at the retention time at 5min (Supplement file, Fig. S1). Calibration curve from the HPLC chromatogram was performed by plotting the peak height against the concentration of the standard paraoxon-ethyl injected into the column. It was found to have a linear correlation relationship of y=10.798x +5.402. There was no significant signal obtained from HPLC when the extract of the reference Bok Choy was tested, indicating that the initial Bok Choy obtained from the market has low or no paraoxon-ethyl. The extract from the spiked leaves was injected to the optimised HPLC method and the concentration of paraoxon-ethyl was evaluated to have the concentration of 0.326±0.051μM (n=3) using the established relationship at retention time at 5min. Using the same batch of extract, the single-shot probe was also tested and ΔI of 3.223±0.001 was recorded (n=3). From the calibration curve showed in inset Fig. 7, the concentration of the paraoxon-ethyl was calculated to be 0.310±0.030μM (n=3). By comparison, the probe method achieved approximately 94.8% accuracy when compared to the conventional HPLC method. However, the recovery of the spiked concentration on the leaves was found to be just 0.02%, with 99.98% loss from the initial spike. This is expected as paraoxon-ethyl is considered quite volatile and the leaves were left in the fumehood for overnight where evaporation can take place. This is to mimic the open system when pesticides are sprayed on crops such as vegetables, fruits and grains at the farms. Although the loss is around 99.98%, the probe suggested in this study was able to capture the presence of paraoxon-ethyl with good accuracy.

This work has successfully demonstrated the development of a ‘single-shot’ probe for the detection of paraoxon-ethyl, a commonly used pesticide on crops. CDs were used as the signalling agent for the detection process, which was synthesised in house using simple acid carbonisation of glucose. The probe has been tested with real vegetable sample and the validation from conventional HPLC showed that the probe can achieve high accuracy even on low concentration sample. Having simple and economical way to produce CDs and the easy protocol to detect the presence of paraoxon-ethyl, the probe might offer a good option to be introduced into real on-site applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to extend our gratitude to the financial support provided by Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus through the Swinburne Sarawak Research Grant, Phase 1/2013 552(2-5509). Special thanks to Ms. Jessica Fong and Ms. Ng Yann Huey for their kind help in the study.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Melissa May Fung CHANG completed her Bachelor of Science (B.Sc.) in Biotechnology in 2014 and Master of Science (M.Sc) by research in 2016, both from Swinburne University of Technology. Her research interest covers the production, characterisation and application of optical nanoparticles, especially carbon nanoparticles for the utilisation in sensing application. She is currently working as a Research Officer in Universiti Malaysia Sarawak, Malaysia.

Irine Runnie Ginjom received her Bachelor of Science (Hons) (B.Sc.) in Agriculture and Food Sciences from the University of Nottingham, UK in 1997 and her Master of Science (M.Sc.) in Food Chemistry and Biochemistry from Universiti Putra Malaysia in 2003. She obtained her Ph.D degree from University of Queensland, Australia, working in the area of Food Science. She is currently a Lecture at Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus and her research interests involve natural products, food technology, food additives and contaminants.

Sing Muk Ng obtained his first degree in Bachelor of Science (Hons) (B.Sc.) in Pure Chemistry from the Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia in 2004. He then pursued his Ph.D degree from The University of Manchester, UK and graduated in 2007, working in the area of optical sensor technology. His current research interests include development of bio/chemical optical sensors and light instrumentations for chemical sensing applications. Previously he worked as research assistant and post-doctoral researcher in these areas at The University of Manchester, UK and Kyungpook National University, Korea. Currently, he is the Associate Director (Science) at the Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre of Sustainable Technologies and Senior Lecturer at the Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Malaysia.
